title: Interaction-driven dynamics in graphene flakes as a benchmark for quantum simulation
authors: Fabian Eickhoff, Satoshi Ejima, Lukas Windgätter, Florian G. Eich, Hannah Rittich, Sebastian Zanker, Peter Schmitteckert
abstract: We study interaction-driven ultrafast dynamics in finite graphene flakes following an optical pump quench in an interacting tight-binding model. By comparing exact real-time evolution with simulations restricted to particle-hole excitation subspaces, we assess when relaxation can be captured by low-order many-body processes and when this is not sufficient. The single-particle orbital entropy provides a compact diagnostic for dynamic correlation growth. For the systems studied here, periodic graphene flakes are well described by low-order excitations, whereas confined geometries require substantial higher-order contributions even for relatively small interaction strengths. The quench protocol combines simple initial-state preparation with strongly correlated dynamics, identifying a promising benchmark problem for future quantum-computing simulations.
summary: The paper analyzes ultrafast carrier relaxation in interacting graphene flakes after an optical quench by comparing full time evolution against simplified models restricted to low-order excitations. It establishes that while periodic boundaries allow for good descriptions using few terms, hard-wall confinement necessitates including high-order correlations, providing a crucial benchmark for quantum simulation methods.
main_problem: The study investigates whether interaction-driven ultrafast dynamics (carrier relaxation) following an optical quench in graphene can be accurately described using only low-order particle–hole excitation subspaces.
main_result: Convergence depends critically on geometry: periodic flakes are well-described by low-order excitations, whereas hard-wall confined geometries require substantial higher-order correlation contributions.
method: The authors compare exact real-time evolution (full Fock space) against truncated simulations restricted to subspaces containing up to $n$ particle–hole excitations.
model_or_system: The system is finite graphene flakes modeled by an interacting tight-binding Hamiltonian for spinless fermions. The dynamics are initiated via a single particle–hole excitation quench protocol.
key_observables: Single-particle orbital entropy ($S_{sp}$), which diagnoses dynamic correlation growth, and the occupation of the initially excited level ($	ilde{n}_p$).
important_parameters: Boundary conditions (Periodic vs. Hard-wall), interaction strengths ($U_1, U_2, U_3$), and excitation order truncation limit ($n=1, 2, 3$).
main_assumptions: The study tests the limits of low-order descriptions against full many-particle exact real-time dynamics; convergence is used as a diagnostic criterion for method applicability.
figures_summary: Figures compare time evolution of orbital entropy and excited level occupation across different excitation orders ($n=1, 2, 3$) for both periodic and hard-wall boundary conditions, highlighting the divergence in confined systems.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a benchmark problem by simulating quench dynamics. It systematically compares full Fock-space evolution against increasingly truncated subspaces (low-order excitations), using entropy convergence as the primary diagnostic tool to map out the regime where simple approximations fail due to geometry or confinement.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, quantitative testbed for many theoretical approaches used in non-equilibrium quantum dynamics, particularly highlighting how boundary conditions can fundamentally alter the required complexity of the underlying physics description.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic representation of the 4 × 4 graphene flake used in this work, comprising 32 lattice sites on the honeycomb lattice. The units are in lattice con- stants.

Figure 2: Time evolution after a particle–hole quench in a 4 × 4 graphene flake (32 sites). The upper panels show the orbital entropy Ssp, while the lower panels display the occupation of the initially excited single-particle level n˜p. The left panels corre- spond to periodic boundary conditions and the right panels to hard-wall boundary conditions. Colored curves denote simulations restricted to at most n = 1 (grey), n = 2 (blue), and n = 3 (olive) particle–hole excitations, while black curves repre- sent the full Fock-space dynamics. Periodic systems are already well described by low-order excitation spaces (n ≤2), whereas hard-wall systems require higher-order excitations and exhibit...

Figure 3: Full Fock-space evolution of all single-particle occupations nq following the quench in a 4 × 4 graphene flake. Single-particle indices on the vertical axis are ordered by the non-interacting eigenenergies εℓ. White (black) indicates occupation one (zero). Panel (a) shows periodic boundary conditions and panel (b) hard-wall boundary conditions. Periodic systems show rapid and efficient redistribution of the excitation, while hard-wall systems display slower and more structured dynamics indicative of higher-order processes.

Figure 4: System-size dependence of excitation-space convergence. Time evolution of the orbital entropy for periodic (a–c) and hard-wall (d–f) boundary conditions for systems of increasing size: 4×2 (a,d), 4×3 (b,e), and 4×4 (c,f). Grey, blue, and olive curves correspond to simulations restricted to n = 1, n = 2, and n = 3 particle–hole excitations, respectively. Low-order excitation spaces remain accurate for periodic graphene across system sizes, whereas hard-wall systems show increasing deviations and growing importance of higher-order correlations.

Figure 5: Interaction dependence of excitation-space convergence for a 4 × 4 graphene flake. Time evolution of the orbital entropy is shown for periodic (a–d) and hard-wall (e–h) boundary conditions at increasing nearest-neighbor interaction strength U1 = 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8 (from left to right), with U2 = U3 = 0. Grey, blue, and olive curves correspond to simulations restricted to n = 1, n = 2, and n = 3 particle–hole excitation sectors, respectively. Increasing interaction strength enhances higher-order contributions in all cases, but periodic systems retain near- convergence at low excitation order over a much wider parameter range than hard- wall systems.
